---
title: "Newton's Method and Second-Order Optimization"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Optimization"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article,
# matched to the rest of the Optimization track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

CURVE   = "#2b3a67"    # the objective / true surface
NEWTON  = "#c1121f"    # the Newton step / quadratic model
GD      = "#6c757d"    # gradient descent, for contrast
ACCENT  = "#0a7d4d"    # the optimum / highlights
GREY    = "#6c757d"
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the fourth and final article of the
> Optimization track's **descent spine**, and the one that breaks the dependence on the
> condition number that haunted the previous three. It assumes [OPTM-02 *Minimizing in
> Many Variables*] for the **gradient** $\nabla f$, the **Hessian** $\nabla^2 f$, the
> **Hessian test** (positive-definite $\Rightarrow$ local min), and **quadratic forms**
> $\mathbf{d}^\top H\mathbf{d}$. It assumes [OPTM-06 *Gradient Descent*] for the iterate
> picture and the negative-gradient step, and [OPTM-07 *Step Size and Convergence*] for
> **$L$-smoothness**, the **condition number** $\kappa=L/\mu$, the **linear rate**
> $\rho=1-1/\kappa$, and **backtracking line search** — the machinery this article will
> show second-order methods escape. From the *Geometry of Data / Applied Linear Algebra*
> track it borrows three objects, *used*, not re-derived: **solving a linear system**
> $H\mathbf{p}=-\mathbf{g}$, the **Cholesky / matrix factorization** that does it in
> $O(p^3)$, and **positive-definiteness** of a symmetric matrix.
>
> **What it sets up.** It is **consumed by** Regression [REGR-17] (logistic regression by
> **IRLS**) and Statistical Inference [INFR-B2]/[INFR-B4] (computing the MLE and
> **Fisher scoring**) — both link here for *Newton's method*. The Gauss–Newton and
> quasi-Newton (BFGS / L-BFGS) material below is the precise tool those articles request.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Gradient descent ([OPTM-06]) sees only the slope at the current point, and [OPTM-07]
showed the price: on an ill-conditioned bowl it zig-zags, and the number of steps to
converge grows linearly with the condition number $\kappa$. This article takes the next
honest step — *use the curvature too.* We build a local **quadratic model** of the
objective from its second-order Taylor expansion, then **jump to that model's exact
minimizer** instead of inching downhill. That jump is the **Newton step**
$$
\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \bigl[\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)\bigr]^{-1}\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k),
$$
and we derive it from scratch — first for root-finding in one dimension ($x-f/f'$), then
for minimization, where the same logic minimizes the quadratic model exactly. We prove its
two signature properties: **quadratic convergence** near the optimum (the number of correct
digits roughly *doubles* each step) and **affine invariance** (it does not see the
conditioning that cripples gradient descent, so $\kappa$ drops out entirely). Then we are
honest about the cost and the failure modes: forming and factoring the Hessian is $O(p^3)$
per step, and a Newton step toward a *non*-positive-definite Hessian can climb instead of
descend — fixed by **damping** (a line search) and **Hessian modification** (trust-region /
Levenberg–Marquardt). Finally we deliver the two workhorses the rest of the series links
here for: **Gauss–Newton** and its statistical twin **IRLS / Fisher scoring** (the exact
algorithm that fits logistic regression and GLMs), and **quasi-Newton** methods (**BFGS**
and **L-BFGS**) that buy most of Newton's speed from gradients alone, never forming a
Hessian. Worked examples carry it end to end: a quadratic where Newton lands in one step, a
logistic-regression fit by IRLS, a Gauss–Newton nonlinear curve fit, and — at the edge of
applicability — a function where the raw Newton step diverges and damping rescues it.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Gradient descent uses only the **slope**. Newton's method also uses the **curvature**: at
the current point it fits the *best local quadratic* — the second-order Taylor model — and
**jumps straight to that quadratic's minimizer**. In one dimension the rule is
$x_{k+1}=x_k - f'(x_k)/f''(x_k)$; in many variables it is
$$
\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - H_k^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k,
\qquad \mathbf{g}_k=\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k),\;\; H_k=\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k).
$$
Two consequences justify the whole method. **(1) Speed:** near a well-behaved minimum the
error is *squared* each step (quadratic convergence) — three or four steps to full machine
precision — and because the step is **affine-invariant**, the condition number $\kappa$ that
governed gradient descent ([OPTM-07]) *drops out entirely*. **(2) Cost and fragility:** each
step forms the $p\times p$ Hessian and solves a linear system, $O(p^3)$, and if $H_k$ is not
positive-definite the "minimizer" of the model can be a *maximizer* — the step heads
uphill. The fixes — **damping** (line-search the Newton direction), **Hessian
modification** (trust regions, Levenberg–Marquardt), and **quasi-Newton** approximations
that never form $H$ at all (BFGS, L-BFGS) — are what make second-order optimization usable
in practice. Its statistical incarnation, **IRLS / Fisher scoring**, is how logistic
regression and every GLM are actually fit.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The single idea — *replace the function locally by a
quadratic and minimize that exactly* — is the engine; quadratic convergence, affine
invariance, the $O(p^3)$ cost, the positive-definiteness trap, Gauss–Newton, and BFGS are
all consequences of it, derived in turn.

# Why we are here: the slope is not the whole story {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **gradient descent throws away the
curvature of the objective — what do we gain by using it, and what does it cost?** Every
derivation below answers a piece of that.

## What OPTM-07 left us holding {#sec-left-holding}

[OPTM-07] gave gradient descent a guarantee and, in the same breath, a disease. On a
strongly convex, $L$-smooth objective the iterates converge **linearly**, the error
multiplied by
$$
\rho = 1 - \frac{\mu}{L} = 1 - \frac{1}{\kappa}
$$
each step, where $\kappa=L/\mu$ is the **condition number** — the ratio of the steepest to
the shallowest curvature. When $\kappa\approx 1$ (a round bowl) descent is nearly instant.
But real objectives are stretched: a ravine with $\kappa=1000$ needs on the order of
$\kappa\ln(1/\varepsilon)\approx 1000\times$ as many steps as a round bowl for the same
accuracy. The iterates zig-zag across the narrow valley because the negative gradient points
mostly *across* the ravine, not *along* it toward the minimum.

Here is the diagnosis [OPTM-07] forced on us, in one sentence: **gradient descent is blind
to the fact that the ravine is a ravine.** It measures the slope, which is steep across the
valley and gentle along it, and dutifully steps in the steep direction — exactly the wrong
one. The slope is a *first-order* object; the shape of the valley — how the slope itself
*changes* as you move — is a *second-order* object. It lives in the **Hessian**, the matrix
of second partial derivatives that [OPTM-02] introduced for the Hessian test. Gradient
descent never looks at it.

So the question writes itself: **if we measured the curvature, could we step in the right
direction instead of the steep one — straight down the ravine to the bottom?** The answer
is yes, and the method that does it is three centuries old.

## A 250-year-old idea, repurposed {#sec-history}

Isaac Newton, around 1669, and Joseph Raphson, in 1690, devised a method not for
*minimizing* but for *solving equations* — finding where a function crosses zero,
$f(x)=0$. Their trick: replace the curve near your current guess by its **tangent line**,
and jump to where the tangent hits zero, because a line is trivial to solve. Repeat. The
tangent is the first-order Taylor model; "jump to where the model is zero" is the whole
method. It converges astonishingly fast — the number of correct digits roughly doubles each
step — and it is the root-finder buried inside every `sqrt`, every `log`, every numerical
library on Earth.

The repurposing for optimization is one observation away. **Minimizing a smooth function $f$
is the same as finding a root of its derivative $f'$.** A smooth interior minimum sits where
$f'(x)=0$ ([OPTM-01]); in many variables, where $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})=\mathbf{0}$
([OPTM-02]). So *apply Newton's root-finder to $f'$* and you get Newton's method for
optimization. The tangent-to-$f'$ picture turns into a **parabola-to-$f$** picture: we model
$f$ itself by a parabola and jump to the parabola's vertex. That is the bridge from
"derivative test" to "second-order optimizer," and we build it in full below.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In one sentence, what is the difference between gradient descent and Newton's method?"*
Gradient descent uses a **linear** (first-order) model of the objective and so can only pick
a *direction*; Newton's method uses a **quadratic** (second-order) model and so picks a
direction *and* the exact step length to that model's minimizer in one shot. Said
differently: gradient descent solves "which way is downhill?"; Newton solves "where is the
bottom of the bowl that best matches the function right here?"
:::

# Intuition before symbols: fit a parabola, jump to its bottom {#sec-intuition}

Before any algebra, the picture. Stand at a point $x_k$ on a smooth curve $f$. Gradient
descent feels the slope $f'(x_k)$ and shuffles a fixed fraction of it downhill, with no idea
how far the bottom is. Newton's method does something bolder: it builds the **unique
parabola** that matches $f$ at $x_k$ in *height, slope, and curvature* — and a parabola has a
vertex you can solve for exactly. So Newton **jumps to the vertex.** If the true function
were that parabola, you would be done in one step. It is not, exactly — so you re-fit a new
parabola at the new point and jump again. Near a minimum the function looks more and more
like its matching parabola, which is precisely why the method gets *spectacularly* accurate
as it closes in.

@fig-parabola draws one Newton step against one gradient step from the same point, so you
can see the difference between "shuffle along the slope" and "jump to the matching
parabola's bottom."

```{python}
#| label: fig-parabola
#| fig-cap: "One step, two philosophies, same start. The objective (navy) is $f(x)=\\tfrac14 x^4 - x^2 + x + 3$. From $x_k=2.1$, **gradient descent** (grey) takes a small step against the slope $f'(x_k)$ and barely moves. **Newton's method** (red) fits the matching parabola $m(x)=f(x_k)+f'(x_k)(x-x_k)+\\tfrac12 f''(x_k)(x-x_k)^2$ — matching $f$ in value, slope, *and* curvature at $x_k$ — and jumps to that parabola's vertex $x_{k+1}=x_k-f'(x_k)/f''(x_k)$, landing far closer to the true minimum (green). The parabola, not the line, is what lets Newton choose a step length."
f   = lambda x: 0.25*x**4 - x**2 + x + 3
fp  = lambda x: x**3 - 2*x + 1
fpp = lambda x: 3*x**2 - 2

xk = 2.1
gk, Hk = fp(xk), fpp(xk)
x_newton = xk - gk/Hk
eta_gd = 0.06
x_gd = xk - eta_gd*gk

xs = np.linspace(-2.4, 2.6, 400)
# matching parabola at xk
m = lambda x: f(xk) + gk*(x-xk) + 0.5*Hk*(x-xk)**2
# true minimizer (right well) for reference
xstar = -2.0
from numpy.polynomial import polynomial as P  # noqa
# locate true min numerically among roots of f'
roots = np.roots([1,0,-2,1])
roots = np.sort(roots[np.isreal(roots)].real)
xstar = roots[np.argmin([f(r) for r in roots])]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(xs, f(xs), color=CURVE, label=r"objective $f$")
ax.plot(xs, m(xs), color=NEWTON, ls="--", lw=1.6, label="matching parabola at $x_k$")
ax.scatter([xk], [f(xk)], color=CURVE, s=55, zorder=5)
ax.annotate("$x_k$", (xk, f(xk)), textcoords="offset points", xytext=(8, 8))
ax.scatter([x_newton], [f(x_newton)], color=NEWTON, s=70, zorder=5, label="Newton lands here")
ax.scatter([x_gd], [f(x_gd)], color=GD, s=70, zorder=5, label="gradient step lands here")
ax.scatter([xstar], [f(xstar)], color=ACCENT, s=90, marker="*", zorder=6, label="true minimum")
ax.set_ylim(0, 9)
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$f(x)$")
ax.set_title("Newton fits a parabola and jumps to its vertex")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The figure already whispers the catch we will confront in @sec-failure: a parabola only has
a *minimum* if it opens **upward**, i.e. if $f''(x_k)>0$. Where the curvature is negative,
the matching parabola opens downward and its vertex is a *maximum* — jump to it and you climb.
Hold that thought; it is the central fragility of the method.

# Formal development I: Newton for root-finding {#sec-rootfinding}

We build the optimizer the way history did: first the root-finder, then turn it on $f'$.

## The one-dimensional root iteration {#sec-1d-root}

We want a solution of $f(x)=0$ for a differentiable $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$. We cannot
solve it directly, so we **linearize**. The first-order Taylor expansion of $f$ about the
current guess $x_k$ — the tangent line — is

$$
f(x) \approx f(x_k) + f'(x_k)\,(x - x_k).
$$ {#eq-tangent}

This is exact for a line and a good approximation near $x_k$ for anything smooth. The
tangent is trivial to solve: set its right-hand side to zero and call the solution
$x_{k+1}$,

$$
f(x_k) + f'(x_k)\,(x_{k+1} - x_k) = 0.
$$

Solve for $x_{k+1}$. Subtract $f(x_k)$, then divide by $f'(x_k)$ (assuming $f'(x_k)\neq 0$ —
a flat tangent never crosses zero, the first failure mode):

$$
x_{k+1} - x_k = -\frac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\boxed{\;x_{k+1} = x_k - \frac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}.\;}
$$ {#eq-newton-root}

That is Newton's root-finding iteration. In words: from your guess, run down the tangent
line to where it crosses zero, and make that your next guess. @fig-rootfinding shows two
steps converging on $\sqrt{2}$ — the root of $f(x)=x^2-2$ — the very computation a hardware
square-root unit performs.

```{python}
#| label: fig-rootfinding
#| fig-cap: "Newton's root-finder solving $x^2-2=0$ (so the root is $\\sqrt 2\\approx 1.4142$). From $x_0=2$, each step replaces the curve (navy) by its tangent (red dashed) and jumps to where the tangent crosses zero (red dots on the axis). Two steps already give $1.41421\\ldots$ — the number of correct digits roughly doubles each iteration. This is the same arithmetic inside a hardware square-root."
f   = lambda x: x**2 - 2
fp  = lambda x: 2*x
xs = np.linspace(0.8, 2.2, 300)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=1.0)
ax.plot(xs, f(xs), color=CURVE, label=r"$f(x)=x^2-2$")
x = 2.0
cols = [NEWTON, "#7a0c14"]
for k in range(2):
    g = fp(x)
    xn = x - f(x)/g
    # tangent line through (x, f(x))
    tx = np.linspace(min(x, xn)-0.15, max(x, xn)+0.15, 50)
    ax.plot(tx, f(x) + g*(tx - x), color=cols[k], ls="--", lw=1.3)
    ax.scatter([x], [f(x)], color=CURVE, s=45, zorder=5)
    ax.scatter([xn], [0], color=cols[k], s=55, zorder=6)
    ax.annotate(f"$x_{k}$", (x, f(x)), textcoords="offset points", xytext=(6, 6))
    x = xn
ax.scatter([np.sqrt(2)], [0], color=ACCENT, s=90, marker="*", zorder=7, label=r"root $\sqrt{2}$")
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$f(x)$")
ax.set_title("Newton root-finding: jump down the tangent to zero")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print("Newton iterates for sqrt(2):")
x = 2.0
for k in range(5):
    print(f"  x_{k} = {x:.15f}   error = {x-np.sqrt(2): .2e}")
    x = x - (x**2-2)/(2*x)
```

The printed errors make the speed concrete: $0.6 \to 0.09 \to 0.002 \to 10^{-6} \to
10^{-12}$. Each error is roughly the *square* of the previous one — the hallmark of
**quadratic convergence**, which we prove in @sec-quadratic.

# Formal development II: Newton for minimization {#sec-minimization}

Now the repurposing. To minimize a smooth $f$, we want $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$. We will derive
the step two equivalent ways — by applying the root-finder to the derivative, and by
minimizing a quadratic model directly — because each illuminates a different half of the
method.

## Route 1 — apply the root-finder to the derivative {#sec-route1}

A smooth interior minimizer of $f$ is a *root of its derivative*: $f'(x^\star)=0$
([OPTM-01]). So set $g(x)=f'(x)$ and run the root iteration @eq-newton-root on $g$. The
derivative of $g$ is $g'(x)=f''(x)$, so substituting $g=f'$ and $g'=f''$ into
@eq-newton-root gives, in one dimension,

$$
\boxed{\;x_{k+1} = x_k - \frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}.\;}
$$ {#eq-newton-1d}

Read it against gradient descent's $x_{k+1}=x_k-\eta f'(x_k)$. The *direction* is the same —
opposite the slope — but the step length is no longer a guessed constant $\eta$. It is
$1/f''(x_k)$: **the reciprocal of the curvature.** Where the function curves sharply (large
$f''$), the step is short and cautious; where it is nearly flat (small $f''$), the step is
long and bold. Newton sizes its own step from the local geometry. That is the whole upgrade,
and @eq-newton-1d already shows the trap: if $f''(x_k)<0$, the step has the *wrong sign* and
climbs (@sec-failure).

## Route 2 — minimize the quadratic model directly {#sec-route2}

The second route generalizes cleanly to many variables and is the one we carry forward.
Instead of linearizing $f'$, we build a **second-order Taylor model of $f$ itself** about
$\mathbf{x}_k$. For $f:\mathbb{R}^p\to\mathbb{R}$, with gradient $\mathbf{g}_k=\nabla
f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ (a $p$-vector) and Hessian $H_k=\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ (the symmetric
$p\times p$ matrix of second partials, from [OPTM-02]), the model is

$$
m_k(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}_k)
  + \mathbf{g}_k^\top(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}_k)
  + \tfrac{1}{2}\,(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}_k)^\top H_k\,(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}_k).
$$ {#eq-quad-model}

This is the multivariable Taylor expansion to second order — the unique quadratic that
matches $f$ in value, gradient, and Hessian at $\mathbf{x}_k$. (The $\tfrac12$ is the same
$\tfrac12$ that sits in front of $f''(a)(x-a)^2$ in the one-variable Taylor formula you know
from Calc 1; it survives the jump to many variables because it comes from integrating twice.)
Let $\mathbf{p}=\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}_k$ be the **step** we are choosing. Rewrite the model
as a function of $\mathbf{p}$:

$$
m_k(\mathbf{p}) = f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \mathbf{g}_k^\top\mathbf{p}
  + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathbf{p}^\top H_k\,\mathbf{p}.
$$ {#eq-quad-in-p}

We minimize this over $\mathbf{p}$ exactly — and here is where we *use*, not assume, the
[OPTM-02] machinery. A quadratic in $\mathbf{p}$ is minimized where its gradient (with
respect to $\mathbf{p}$) vanishes. We need two vector-calculus derivative facts, both proved
in [OPTM-02] from the definition of the gradient; we restate them because the reader is rusty:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{p}}\bigl(\mathbf{g}_k^\top\mathbf{p}\bigr) = \mathbf{g}_k,
\qquad
\nabla_{\mathbf{p}}\bigl(\tfrac12\,\mathbf{p}^\top H_k\,\mathbf{p}\bigr) = H_k\,\mathbf{p}
\quad(\text{since } H_k \text{ is symmetric}).
$$ {#eq-vec-derivs}

The first is the gradient of a linear form: differentiating $\sum_j (g_k)_j p_j$ with respect
to $p_i$ leaves $(g_k)_i$, so stacking gives $\mathbf{g}_k$. The second is the gradient of a
quadratic form: differentiating $\tfrac12\sum_{i,j} p_i (H_k)_{ij} p_j$ with respect to $p_i$
gives $\tfrac12\bigl(\sum_j (H_k)_{ij}p_j + \sum_j (H_k)_{ji}p_j\bigr) = \sum_j (H_k)_{ij}p_j$
when $H_k$ is symmetric ($(H_k)_{ij}=(H_k)_{ji}$, true for any twice-differentiable $f$ by
the equality of mixed partials), i.e. row $i$ of $H_k\mathbf{p}$. Setting the model's
gradient to zero,

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{p}}\, m_k(\mathbf{p}) = \mathbf{g}_k + H_k\,\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{0}.
$$ {#eq-model-stationary}

Solve for the step. Move $\mathbf{g}_k$ over and multiply by the inverse Hessian:

$$
H_k\,\mathbf{p} = -\mathbf{g}_k
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\mathbf{p}^{\text{N}}_k = -H_k^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k.
$$ {#eq-newton-step}

The vector $\mathbf{p}^{\text{N}}_k=-H_k^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k$ is the **Newton step** (or Newton
direction). Adding it to $\mathbf{x}_k$ gives the multivariate Newton iteration:

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - H_k^{-1}\,\mathbf{g}_k
   = \mathbf{x}_k - \bigl[\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)\bigr]^{-1}\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k).\;}
$$ {#eq-newton-multi}

Set $p=1$ and this collapses to @eq-newton-1d: $H_k$ is the single number $f''(x_k)$, its
inverse is $1/f''(x_k)$, and the step is $-f'(x_k)/f''(x_k)$. The two routes agree, as they
must, since "root of $f'$" and "vertex of the quadratic model of $f$" are the same point.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**You never *compute the inverse* $H_k^{-1}$; you *solve the linear system* $H_k\mathbf{p}
=-\mathbf{g}_k$.** Writing $H^{-1}\mathbf{g}$ on paper is fine, but forming the matrix
$H^{-1}$ explicitly and multiplying is slower *and* numerically worse than solving the
system directly (e.g. by a Cholesky factorization when $H$ is positive-definite). In code
the line is `p = np.linalg.solve(H, -g)`, never `p = -np.linalg.inv(H) @ g`. This is the
single most common implementation mistake in a Newton method, and an interviewer will probe
it.
:::

## Reading the step: Newton rescales space {#sec-rescale}

Why is $-H^{-1}\mathbf{g}$ a *better* direction than $-\mathbf{g}$? Because $H^{-1}$ undoes
the stretching of the bowl. Gradient descent steps along $-\mathbf{g}$, which on a stretched
bowl points mostly across the ravine. Multiplying by $H^{-1}$ *divides out the curvature*:
directions of large curvature (the steep walls) are shrunk, directions of small curvature
(the gentle valley floor) are stretched, and what comes out points down the valley toward
the bottom. In the eigenbasis of $H$, with eigenvalues $\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_p$
(the curvatures along the principal axes), the gradient component along axis $j$ is divided
by $\lambda_j$ — so the badly-scaled and well-scaled directions are equalized. The next
section makes this exact: it is *why* the condition number disappears.

# Formal development III: why Newton is fast — quadratic convergence and affine invariance {#sec-fast}

Two theorems justify the method. The first explains the eye-popping speed; the second
explains why $\kappa$ vanishes.

## Quadratic convergence {#sec-quadratic}

::: {#thm-quadratic}
**Theorem (local quadratic convergence).** Let $f$ be twice continuously differentiable with
a minimizer $\mathbf{x}^\star$ at which the Hessian $H^\star=\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^\star)$ is
positive-definite (so $H^\star$ is invertible), and suppose the Hessian is **Lipschitz** near
$\mathbf{x}^\star$: $\|\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x})-\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{y})\|\le M\|\mathbf{x}
-\mathbf{y}\|$ for some constant $M$. Then for $\mathbf{x}_k$ close enough to
$\mathbf{x}^\star$, the Newton iterate @eq-newton-multi satisfies
$$
\|\mathbf{x}_{k+1}-\mathbf{x}^\star\| \;\le\; C\,\|\mathbf{x}_k-\mathbf{x}^\star\|^2
$$
for a constant $C$. The error is squared each step: convergence is **quadratic**.
:::

::: {.proof}
Write the error $\mathbf{e}_k=\mathbf{x}_k-\mathbf{x}^\star$. We track how
$\mathbf{e}_{k+1}$ depends on $\mathbf{e}_k$. Start from the iteration and subtract
$\mathbf{x}^\star$ from both sides:

$$
\mathbf{e}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_{k+1}-\mathbf{x}^\star
= \mathbf{x}_k - H_k^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k - \mathbf{x}^\star
= \mathbf{e}_k - H_k^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k.
$$

Now factor out $H_k^{-1}$ (it is invertible near $\mathbf{x}^\star$, since $H^\star$ is and
the Hessian varies continuously), writing $\mathbf{e}_k = H_k^{-1}H_k\mathbf{e}_k$:

$$
\mathbf{e}_{k+1} = H_k^{-1}\bigl(H_k\,\mathbf{e}_k - \mathbf{g}_k\bigr).
$$ {#eq-err-recur}

Everything now rides on the quantity in parentheses, $H_k\mathbf{e}_k-\mathbf{g}_k$. We
evaluate it using the **fundamental theorem of calculus for the gradient** — the multivariate
version of "the change in a function is the integral of its derivative," a Calc-1 idea lifted
to vectors. Because $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)=\mathbf{0}$, the gradient at $\mathbf{x}_k$
is the *accumulated change in the gradient* from $\mathbf{x}^\star$ to $\mathbf{x}_k$, and
the rate of change of the gradient is the Hessian:

$$
\mathbf{g}_k = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) - \underbrace{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)}_{=\,\mathbf{0}}
= \int_0^1 \nabla^2 f\bigl(\mathbf{x}^\star + t\,\mathbf{e}_k\bigr)\,\mathbf{e}_k\,dt.
$$ {#eq-ftc-grad}

(The integrand is the Hessian at a point sliding from $\mathbf{x}^\star$ to $\mathbf{x}_k$,
times the direction $\mathbf{e}_k$; integrating it from $0$ to $1$ accumulates the total
change in $\nabla f$ along that segment. This is exactly $\int_a^b g'(t)\,dt = g(b)-g(a)$,
applied component-wise to $\nabla f$.) Since $\mathbf{e}_k$ does not depend on $t$, we may
also write the *constant* term $H_k\mathbf{e}_k = \int_0^1 H_k\,\mathbf{e}_k\,dt$. Subtract:

$$
H_k\,\mathbf{e}_k - \mathbf{g}_k
= \int_0^1 \Bigl[H_k - \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^\star + t\,\mathbf{e}_k)\Bigr]\,\mathbf{e}_k\,dt.
$$ {#eq-diff-integral}

Now bound the norm. Take norms in @eq-err-recur and @eq-diff-integral, using
$\|A\mathbf{v}\|\le\|A\|\,\|\mathbf{v}\|$ and that the norm of an integral is at most the
integral of the norm:

$$
\|\mathbf{e}_{k+1}\| \le \|H_k^{-1}\|\int_0^1
   \bigl\|H_k - \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^\star+t\mathbf{e}_k)\bigr\|\;\|\mathbf{e}_k\|\,dt.
$$

Apply the Lipschitz bound to the matrix difference. The two Hessians are evaluated at
$\mathbf{x}_k$ (recall $H_k=\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)$) and at $\mathbf{x}^\star+t\mathbf{e}_k$,
which are a distance $\|\mathbf{x}_k - (\mathbf{x}^\star+t\mathbf{e}_k)\| =
\|(1-t)\mathbf{e}_k\| = (1-t)\|\mathbf{e}_k\|$ apart. So

$$
\bigl\|H_k - \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^\star+t\mathbf{e}_k)\bigr\| \le M(1-t)\|\mathbf{e}_k\|.
$$

Substitute and pull the $\mathbf{e}_k$ factors out of the integral (they do not depend on
$t$ except through the $(1-t)$ we just produced):

$$
\|\mathbf{e}_{k+1}\| \le \|H_k^{-1}\|\,M\,\|\mathbf{e}_k\|^2 \int_0^1 (1-t)\,dt
= \|H_k^{-1}\|\,M\,\|\mathbf{e}_k\|^2 \cdot \tfrac12.
$$

The integral $\int_0^1(1-t)\,dt = \tfrac12$ is elementary Calc 1. Finally, near
$\mathbf{x}^\star$ the inverse Hessian norm $\|H_k^{-1}\|$ is bounded by a constant (it
approaches $\|(H^\star)^{-1}\|$ by continuity), so collecting constants into
$C=\tfrac{M}{2}\sup\|H_k^{-1}\|$,

$$
\|\mathbf{e}_{k+1}\| \le C\,\|\mathbf{e}_k\|^2,
$$

which is exactly the claim. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The inequality $\|\mathbf{e}_{k+1}\|\le C\|\mathbf{e}_k\|^2$ is the engine of Newton's
reputation. Once $C\|\mathbf{e}_k\|<1$, the error not only shrinks but the *number of correct
digits doubles* each step: an error of $10^{-2}$ becomes $\sim 10^{-4}$, then $\sim 10^{-8}$,
then $\sim 10^{-16}$ — machine precision in three steps. Contrast the *linear* rate of
gradient descent, $\|\mathbf{e}_{k+1}\|\le\rho\|\mathbf{e}_k\|$, which gains a *fixed* number
of digits per step. @fig-rates shows the two on the same plot.

```{python}
#| label: fig-rates
#| fig-cap: "Linear versus quadratic convergence, on a log scale, minimizing $f(x,y)=\\tfrac12(x^2 + 25 y^2)$ — an ill-conditioned bowl with $\\kappa=25$. Gradient descent (grey) descends in a straight line on the log plot, gaining a fixed number of digits per step at a rate set by $\\kappa$. Newton's method (red) needs *one* step: the objective is exactly quadratic, so its quadratic model is exact and the vertex is the true minimum. On a non-quadratic objective Newton would curve downward ever more steeply — the error squaring each step — but here it is immediate."
A = np.array([[1.0, 0.0],[0.0, 25.0]])   # Hessian, kappa = 25
grad = lambda x: A @ x
xstar = np.zeros(2)

x0 = np.array([5.0, 1.0])
# Gradient descent with optimal constant step eta = 2/(L+mu)
L, mu = 25.0, 1.0
eta = 2.0/(L+mu)
x = x0.copy(); gd_err = [np.linalg.norm(x-xstar)]
for _ in range(60):
    x = x - eta*grad(x)
    gd_err.append(np.linalg.norm(x-xstar))

# Newton: x - H^{-1} g ; H = A constant
x = x0.copy(); nt_err = [np.linalg.norm(x-xstar)]
for _ in range(5):
    g = grad(x)
    p = np.linalg.solve(A, -g)
    x = x + p
    nt_err.append(np.linalg.norm(x-xstar))

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.semilogy(range(len(gd_err)), np.maximum(gd_err, 1e-18), color=GD,
            marker="o", ms=3, label=r"gradient descent ($\kappa=25$)")
ax.semilogy(range(len(nt_err)), np.maximum(nt_err, 1e-18), color=NEWTON,
            marker="s", ms=5, label="Newton's method")
ax.set_xlabel("iteration $k$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"error $\|x_k - x^\star\|$")
ax.set_title("Linear (GD) vs. quadratic (Newton) convergence")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", framealpha=0.9)
ax.set_ylim(1e-17, 10)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Affine invariance: where the condition number goes {#sec-affine}

The deeper reason Newton ignores conditioning is **affine invariance**. The claim: Newton's
iterates are unchanged (up to the coordinate map) if you rescale or rotate the variables by
any invertible linear transformation. Gradient descent has no such property — rescaling the
axes changes its entire trajectory, which is precisely why a stretched bowl ($\kappa$ large)
defeats it.

::: {#thm-affine}
**Theorem (affine invariance of Newton's method).** Let $A$ be an invertible $p\times p$
matrix and consider the change of variables $\mathbf{x}=A\mathbf{y}$, with
$\tilde f(\mathbf{y})=f(A\mathbf{y})$. If $\{\mathbf{x}_k\}$ are the Newton iterates of $f$
and $\{\mathbf{y}_k\}$ the Newton iterates of $\tilde f$ started at the corresponding point
$\mathbf{y}_0 = A^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0$, then $\mathbf{x}_k = A\mathbf{y}_k$ for all $k$.
:::

::: {.proof}
We need the gradient and Hessian of $\tilde f$ in terms of those of $f$ — the multivariable
chain rule, which we apply explicitly. With $\mathbf{x}=A\mathbf{y}$, the chain rule gives the
gradient (treating $\nabla$ as a column vector, the $A^\top$ comes from differentiating
$A\mathbf{y}$ with respect to $\mathbf{y}$):

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{y}}\tilde f(\mathbf{y}) = A^\top \nabla f(A\mathbf{y}).
$$

Differentiating once more — the Hessian transforms with an $A$ on each side:

$$
\nabla^2_{\mathbf{y}}\tilde f(\mathbf{y}) = A^\top \nabla^2 f(A\mathbf{y})\,A.
$$

Now compute the Newton step for $\tilde f$ at $\mathbf{y}_k$, abbreviating
$\mathbf{g}=\nabla f(A\mathbf{y}_k)$ and $H=\nabla^2 f(A\mathbf{y}_k)$ (the gradient and
Hessian of the *original* $f$ at the corresponding point $\mathbf{x}_k=A\mathbf{y}_k$):

$$
\mathbf{y}_{k+1} = \mathbf{y}_k - \bigl[\nabla^2_{\mathbf{y}}\tilde f\bigr]^{-1}
   \nabla_{\mathbf{y}}\tilde f
= \mathbf{y}_k - \bigl(A^\top H A\bigr)^{-1} A^\top \mathbf{g}.
$$

Use the inverse-of-a-product rule $(A^\top H A)^{-1} = A^{-1}H^{-1}(A^\top)^{-1}$ (valid since
all three factors are invertible). The trailing $(A^\top)^{-1}A^\top$ cancels:

$$
\mathbf{y}_{k+1} = \mathbf{y}_k - A^{-1}H^{-1}(A^\top)^{-1}A^\top \mathbf{g}
= \mathbf{y}_k - A^{-1}H^{-1}\mathbf{g}.
$$

Multiply both sides by $A$ on the left, and use the inductive hypothesis
$A\mathbf{y}_k=\mathbf{x}_k$:

$$
A\mathbf{y}_{k+1} = A\mathbf{y}_k - H^{-1}\mathbf{g}
= \mathbf{x}_k - H^{-1}\mathbf{g} = \mathbf{x}_{k+1},
$$

the last equality being exactly the Newton iteration for $f$ (@eq-newton-multi). So
$A\mathbf{y}_{k+1}=\mathbf{x}_{k+1}$, completing the induction. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the payoff. Stretching the bowl is precisely an affine change of variables, and
Newton's trajectory is invariant under it — so the condition number $\kappa$, which is a
*property of the scaling*, **cannot appear in Newton's convergence**. Where gradient descent's
iteration count scales like $\kappa$, Newton's does not depend on $\kappa$ at all. The
quadratic-convergence constant $C$ depends on the Hessian's *Lipschitz* constant $M$ (how
fast the curvature changes), not on its conditioning. This is the single most important
structural advantage of second-order methods, and it is why a well-implemented Newton method
crushes gradient descent on hard, stretched problems.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Gradient descent's speed depends on the condition number; why doesn't Newton's?"* Because
Newton is **affine-invariant** (@thm-affine): a linear rescaling of the variables leaves its
iterates unchanged up to that rescaling. The condition number is exactly a measure of how
badly the variables are scaled relative to each other — it is an artifact of the coordinate
system. Gradient descent steps along $-\nabla f$, a direction that *is* coordinate-dependent,
so it sees the scaling. Newton steps along $-H^{-1}\nabla f$, which automatically rescales by
the inverse curvature, undoing the conditioning. In one line: **Newton optimizes in the
"natural" coordinates of the problem, gradient descent in whatever coordinates you happened to
write it in.**
:::

# Formal development IV: the costs and the failure modes {#sec-failure}

Newton's two gifts come with two bills. This is the section that separates "knows the
formula" from "knows when not to use it."

## The cost: forming and factoring the Hessian {#sec-cost}

Each Newton step does three things gradient descent does not: it forms the $p\times p$
Hessian (up to $p(p+1)/2$ distinct second partials), and it solves the linear system
$H_k\mathbf{p}=-\mathbf{g}_k$. Solving a dense $p\times p$ system costs $O(p^3)$ arithmetic
operations (e.g. a Cholesky factorization, $\tfrac13 p^3$ flops). Storing the Hessian costs
$O(p^2)$ memory. Compare gradient descent: $O(p)$ work and $O(p)$ memory per step.

For $p$ in the dozens or low thousands this is a bargain — Newton's tiny iteration count
swamps the per-step cost. But for a deep network with $p=10^9$ parameters, an $O(p^3)$ solve
and an $O(p^2)$ matrix are both flatly impossible: $p^2 = 10^{18}$ numbers will not fit in any
memory. **This is the headline reason Newton's method is not used to train large neural
networks** — and the reason quasi-Newton methods (@sec-quasinewton) and first-order methods
(gradient descent, SGD, Adam — [OPTM-06], [OPTM-11], [OPTM-12]) dominate that regime. The
honest rule of thumb: Newton and its quasi-Newton cousins own the small-to-medium-scale,
smooth, accuracy-hungry world ($p$ up to $\sim 10^4$); first-order methods own the
large-scale, noisy, deep-learning world.

## The fragility: a non-positive-definite Hessian {#sec-fragility}

Recall the parabola picture (@sec-intuition): the matching parabola has a *minimum* only if
it opens upward. In many variables, "opens upward in every direction" is exactly
**$H_k$ positive-definite** — $\mathbf{d}^\top H_k\mathbf{d}>0$ for all $\mathbf{d}\neq\mathbf{0}$
(the Hessian test of [OPTM-02]). When $H_k$ is *not* positive-definite, two things break:

1. **The step can be uphill.** If $H_k$ has a negative eigenvalue, the quadratic model
   @eq-quad-in-p has a *saddle* or a *maximum*, not a minimum, and "jump to the model's
   stationary point" can *increase* $f$. The clean test: the Newton step is a descent
   direction iff $\mathbf{g}_k^\top\mathbf{p}^{\text{N}}_k<0$. Substitute
   $\mathbf{p}^{\text{N}}_k=-H_k^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k$:
   $$
   \mathbf{g}_k^\top \mathbf{p}^{\text{N}}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k^\top H_k^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k,
   $$
   which is negative — a genuine descent direction — *exactly when* $H_k^{-1}$ (equivalently
   $H_k$) is positive-definite. If $H_k$ has a negative eigenvalue, this can be positive and
   Newton points uphill.
2. **The step can blow up.** If $H_k$ is *singular* (a zero eigenvalue — a flat direction),
   $H_k^{-1}$ does not exist and the step is infinite. Near-singular $H_k$ gives an enormous,
   unreliable step. This is the multivariate echo of @eq-newton-1d dividing by $f''(x_k)\approx
   0$.

@fig-divergence shows the raw Newton iteration *diverging* on a function whose curvature goes
the wrong way, and the damped version (next subsection) converging on the same function.

```{python}
#| label: fig-divergence
#| fig-cap: "Raw Newton can diverge. Minimizing $f(x)=\\log(1+e^{-3x}) + 0.05x^2$ — convex, with a unique minimum near $x\\approx 1.1$ — from $x_0=2.5$ where the curvature $f''$ is tiny. **Grey:** the *undamped* Newton step $x-f'/f''$ overshoots wildly (the near-flat region gives a huge step) and oscillates away. **Red:** the *damped* Newton step $x-tf'/f''$ with a backtracking line search on $t$ stays a descent method and converges. Damping is what makes Newton safe far from the optimum."
import numpy as np
def f(x):   return np.log1p(np.exp(-3*x)) + 0.05*x**2
def fp(x):
    s = 1/(1+np.exp(3*x))      # sigmoid(-3x), stable
    return -3*s + 0.1*x
def fpp(x):
    s = 1/(1+np.exp(3*x))
    return 9*s*(1-s) + 0.1

xs = np.linspace(-1, 3, 300)

# Undamped Newton
x = 2.5; raw = [x]
for _ in range(8):
    x = x - fp(x)/fpp(x)
    raw.append(x)
    if abs(x) > 50: break

# Damped Newton with backtracking on t
x = 2.5; damped = [x]
for _ in range(12):
    step = -fp(x)/fpp(x)
    t = 1.0
    while f(x + t*step) > f(x) + 1e-4*t*fp(x)*step and t > 1e-6:
        t *= 0.5
    x = x + t*step
    damped.append(x)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
axL.plot(xs, f(xs), color=CURVE)
ry = [f(v) if abs(v) < 50 else np.nan for v in raw]
axL.plot(raw[:6], ry[:6], color=GD, marker="o", ms=4, lw=1.2)
axL.set_title("Undamped Newton overshoots")
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$f(x)$"); axL.set_xlim(-1, 3)
axL.set_ylim(0, 3)

axR.plot(xs, f(xs), color=CURVE)
axR.plot(damped, [f(v) for v in damped], color=NEWTON, marker="s", ms=4, lw=1.2)
axR.set_title("Damped Newton converges")
axR.set_xlabel("$x$"); axR.set_xlim(-1, 3); axR.set_ylim(0, 3)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"undamped iterates (first 6): {[round(v,2) for v in raw[:6]]}")
print(f"damped   iterates (last 4):  {[round(v,4) for v in damped[-4:]]}")
```

## The fixes: damping and Hessian modification {#sec-fixes}

Two standard repairs make Newton globally usable, not just locally:

**Damping (line search).** Do not commit to the full step. Take the Newton *direction*
$\mathbf{p}^{\text{N}}_k=-H_k^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k$ but scale it by a step length $t_k\in(0,1]$
chosen by the same **backtracking line search** of [OPTM-07]: start at $t=1$ (the full Newton
step) and halve until the Armijo sufficient-decrease condition holds,
$$
f(\mathbf{x}_k + t\,\mathbf{p}^{\text{N}}_k) \le f(\mathbf{x}_k)
   + c\,t\,\mathbf{g}_k^\top\mathbf{p}^{\text{N}}_k, \qquad c\in(0,\tfrac12).
$$
Far from the optimum this shortens overlong steps and guarantees descent; *near* the optimum
the full step $t=1$ is always accepted, so quadratic convergence is preserved. This two-phase
behavior — globally safe, locally quadratic — is the **damped (guarded) Newton method**, the
version you actually run.

**Hessian modification.** When $H_k$ is not positive-definite, the direction itself is
suspect, so *fix the matrix* before solving. Replace $H_k$ with $H_k+\tau I$ for a $\tau\ge 0$
just large enough to make it positive-definite (every eigenvalue is shifted up by $\tau$, so
$\tau>-\lambda_{\min}(H_k)$ suffices). This is **Levenberg–Marquardt damping**, and it
interpolates between the two methods: $\tau=0$ is pure Newton, and as $\tau\to\infty$ the
step $-(H_k+\tau I)^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k\approx -\tfrac1\tau\mathbf{g}_k$ becomes a *tiny gradient
step.* The closely-related **trust-region** view chooses the step by minimizing the quadratic
model *subject to staying within a radius* where the model is trusted, which automatically
controls both the bad-curvature and the too-large-step pathologies. We do not derive the
trust-region subproblem here — it is a constrained optimization, the subject of the
constrained branch ([OPTM-13]–[OPTM-14]); we flag it as the principled cousin of damping.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What can go wrong with Newton's method, and how do you guard against each failure?"* Three
things, and a fix for each. **(1) The Hessian is not positive-definite** far from the optimum,
so the step may go uphill — guard with **Hessian modification** ($H+\tau I$, Levenberg–
Marquardt, or a trust region). **(2) The full step overshoots** — guard with **damping**, a
backtracking line search on the step length. **(3) The Hessian is too big or too expensive**
($O(p^2)$ memory, $O(p^3)$ per solve) — guard by *not forming it*: use a **quasi-Newton**
approximation (BFGS / L-BFGS) or **Gauss–Newton** when the objective is a sum of squares. A
strong answer names the failure *and* the matching remedy.
:::

# Gauss–Newton and IRLS: the algorithm the rest of the series links here for {#sec-gaussnewton}

Two enormous classes of problems — **nonlinear least squares** and **maximum likelihood for
GLMs** — have so much structure that we can get a Newton-quality step *without ever computing
the true Hessian*. This is the precise tool Regression's logistic/GLM articles and
Inference's MLE articles request, so we derive it carefully.

## Gauss–Newton for nonlinear least squares {#sec-gn}

Suppose the objective is a **sum of squared residuals**,
$$
f(\boldsymbol\theta) = \tfrac12\sum_{i=1}^n r_i(\boldsymbol\theta)^2
= \tfrac12\,\mathbf{r}(\boldsymbol\theta)^\top\mathbf{r}(\boldsymbol\theta),
$$ {#eq-nls}
where $r_i(\boldsymbol\theta) = y_i - \hat y_i(\boldsymbol\theta)$ is the $i$-th residual and
the model $\hat y_i$ may depend *nonlinearly* on the parameters $\boldsymbol\theta\in\mathbb{R}^p$
(if it were linear, this would be ordinary least squares with a closed form — [REGR-01]).
Stack the residuals into the vector $\mathbf{r}\in\mathbb{R}^n$ and let
$J\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times p}$ be its **Jacobian**, $J_{ij}=\partial r_i/\partial\theta_j$.

Compute the exact gradient and Hessian by differentiating @eq-nls. The gradient (product
rule, $\nabla\tfrac12\|\mathbf{r}\|^2 = J^\top\mathbf{r}$):
$$
\nabla f = \sum_{i=1}^n r_i\,\nabla r_i = J^\top\mathbf{r}.
$$
Differentiating once more for the Hessian, the product rule gives **two** terms:
$$
\nabla^2 f = \underbrace{J^\top J}_{\text{first-order}}
  \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n r_i\,\nabla^2 r_i}_{\text{second-order, curvature of the model}}.
$$ {#eq-nls-hessian}

The first term $J^\top J$ uses only first derivatives (the Jacobian); the second term needs
the second derivatives $\nabla^2 r_i$ of every residual — expensive, and exactly what we want
to avoid. **The Gauss–Newton approximation drops the second term**, replacing the Hessian by
$J^\top J$:
$$
\boxed{\;\boldsymbol\theta_{k+1} = \boldsymbol\theta_k - (J_k^\top J_k)^{-1} J_k^\top\mathbf{r}_k.\;}
$$ {#eq-gn-step}

When is dropping $\sum r_i\nabla^2 r_i$ justified? Read the term: it is the residuals $r_i$
*weighting* the model's curvature. Two cases make it negligible. **(i) Small residuals:** near
a good fit the $r_i$ are tiny, so the dropped term is tiny and Gauss–Newton $\approx$ Newton —
it inherits near-quadratic convergence. **(ii) Nearly linear model:** if each $r_i$ is almost
linear in $\boldsymbol\theta$, then $\nabla^2 r_i\approx 0$ and the term vanishes regardless of
residual size. When residuals are large *and* the model is sharply curved, Gauss–Newton
degrades — that is its honest boundary, and **Levenberg–Marquardt** (replace $J^\top J$ by
$J^\top J + \tau I$, @sec-fixes) is the standard repair, which is why LM is *the* workhorse of
nonlinear curve fitting.

Notice the bonus: $J^\top J$ is **always positive-semidefinite** ($\mathbf{d}^\top J^\top
J\mathbf{d}=\|J\mathbf{d}\|^2\ge 0$), so the positive-definiteness fragility of @sec-fragility
mostly evaporates — Gauss–Newton steps are descent directions whenever $J$ has full column
rank. And the step @eq-gn-step is exactly the normal-equations solution of a *linear* least-
squares problem, $\min_{\mathbf{p}}\|J_k\mathbf{p}+\mathbf{r}_k\|^2$ — so each Gauss–Newton
iteration is "solve a linear regression on the current Jacobian," the structure that makes it
both fast and numerically clean.

```{python}
#| label: fig-gaussnewton
#| fig-cap: "Gauss–Newton fitting a nonlinear model $\\hat y = a\\,e^{bx}$ to noisy exponential-decay data ($n=40$) — a genuine nonlinear least-squares problem with no closed form. **Left:** the converged fit (red) through the data (navy). **Right:** the objective $\\tfrac12\\|\\mathbf r\\|^2$ versus iteration on a log scale; Gauss–Newton reaches the minimum in a handful of steps, far faster than the gradient-descent baseline (grey)."
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
x = np.linspace(0, 4, 40)
a_true, b_true = 5.0, -0.8
y = a_true*np.exp(b_true*x) + rng.normal(0, 0.15, size=x.size)

def resid(theta):
    a, b = theta
    return (a*np.exp(b*x)) - y          # r_i = yhat - y_i
def jac(theta):
    a, b = theta
    e = np.exp(b*x)
    return np.column_stack([e, a*x*e])  # d r / d a , d r / d b
def obj(theta):
    return 0.5*np.sum(resid(theta)**2)

theta = np.array([1.0, -0.1])           # poor start
gn_hist = [obj(theta)]
for _ in range(12):
    r = resid(theta); J = jac(theta)
    p = np.linalg.solve(J.T@J, -J.T@r)  # Gauss-Newton step
    # light damping for safety far from optimum
    t = 1.0
    while obj(theta + t*p) > gn_hist[-1] and t > 1e-4:
        t *= 0.5
    theta = theta + t*p
    gn_hist.append(obj(theta))

# Gradient-descent baseline
th = np.array([1.0, -0.1]); gd_hist = [obj(th)]
eta = 2e-3
for _ in range(400):
    r = resid(th); J = jac(th)
    th = th - eta*(J.T@r)
    gd_hist.append(obj(th))

xs = np.linspace(0, 4, 200)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
axL.scatter(x, y, color=CURVE, s=28, label="data")
axL.plot(xs, theta[0]*np.exp(theta[1]*xs), color=NEWTON,
         label=fr"fit $a={theta[0]:.2f},\ b={theta[1]:.2f}$")
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$y$"); axL.set_title("Nonlinear least-squares fit")
axL.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.semilogy(range(len(gn_hist)), gn_hist, color=NEWTON, marker="s", ms=4,
             label="Gauss-Newton")
axR.semilogy(range(len(gd_hist)), gd_hist, color=GD, lw=1.4, label="gradient descent")
axR.set_xlabel("iteration"); axR.set_ylabel(r"objective $\frac{1}{2}\,\|r\|^2$")
axR.set_title("Convergence: GN vs. GD")
axR.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"Gauss-Newton converged in ~{len(gn_hist)-1} steps to a={theta[0]:.3f}, b={theta[1]:.3f}")
print(f"(true a={a_true}, b={b_true})")
```

## IRLS / Fisher scoring: Newton on a log-likelihood {#sec-irls}

The same idea, applied to *maximum likelihood*, produces the algorithm that fits logistic
regression and every generalized linear model. Statistical Inference owns *what* the MLE is
([INFR-B2]); we own *how to compute it*. The MLE maximizes the log-likelihood
$\ell(\boldsymbol\beta)$, equivalently minimizes $-\ell$, so it solves the **score equation**
$\nabla\ell(\boldsymbol\beta)=\mathbf{0}$ — a root-finding problem, Newton's home turf. The
Newton update for *maximizing* $\ell$ (note the sign: maximizing flips the step direction)
is
$$
\boldsymbol\beta_{k+1} = \boldsymbol\beta_k - \bigl[\nabla^2\ell(\boldsymbol\beta_k)\bigr]^{-1}
   \nabla\ell(\boldsymbol\beta_k).
$$
**Fisher scoring** makes one substitution: replace the (random, observation-dependent)
Hessian $\nabla^2\ell$ by its expectation, the negative **Fisher information**
$-\mathcal{I}(\boldsymbol\beta)=\mathbb{E}[\nabla^2\ell]$. This is the exact analogue of the
Gauss–Newton move — swap the true second derivative for a cheaper, guaranteed-definite
surrogate:
$$
\boldsymbol\beta_{k+1} = \boldsymbol\beta_k + \mathcal{I}(\boldsymbol\beta_k)^{-1}
   \nabla\ell(\boldsymbol\beta_k).
$$
For a GLM this update algebraically *rearranges* into a **weighted least-squares** problem: at
each step you solve $\min_{\boldsymbol\beta}\sum_i w_i(z_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top\boldsymbol\beta)^2$
for a "working response" $z_i$ and weights $w_i$ that are recomputed each iteration — hence the
name **Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS)**. The detailed GLM derivation (the working
response, the weights, the link function) belongs to the model — it is owned by Regression
[REGR-17] and Inference [INFR-B4], which import the *Newton/Fisher-scoring engine* from here.
The logistic example below shows the engine running.

```{python}
#| label: fig-irls
#| fig-cap: "Logistic regression fit by Newton's method (IRLS). The data are $n=120$ points with a binary outcome whose probability rises with $x$; the model is $P(y=1\\mid x)=\\sigma(\\beta_0+\\beta_1 x)$. **Left:** the fitted probability curve (red) through the 0/1 data (navy), with the decision boundary $\\hat P=0.5$ marked. **Right:** the negative log-likelihood versus Newton iteration — it reaches the minimum in 5–6 steps, the quadratic convergence of @thm-quadratic on a real statistical objective."
rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
n = 120
x = rng.uniform(-3, 3, n)
p_true = 1/(1+np.exp(-(0.7 + 1.4*x)))
y = (rng.uniform(size=n) < p_true).astype(float)
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), x])     # design matrix, intercept + slope

def sigmoid(z): return 1/(1+np.exp(-z))
def negll(beta):
    z = X @ beta
    # stable log(1+e^z) form
    return np.sum(np.logaddexp(0, z) - y*z)

beta = np.zeros(2)
hist = [negll(beta)]
for _ in range(8):
    z = X @ beta
    p = sigmoid(z)
    grad = X.T @ (p - y)                 # gradient of negative log-lik
    W = p*(1-p)                          # IRLS weights
    H = X.T @ (W[:,None]*X)              # Hessian = X^T W X (Fisher info)
    step = np.linalg.solve(H, -grad)     # Newton/IRLS step
    beta = beta + step
    hist.append(negll(beta))

xs = np.linspace(-3, 3, 200)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
axL.scatter(x, y, color=CURVE, s=22, alpha=0.7, label="data (0/1)")
axL.plot(xs, sigmoid(beta[0] + beta[1]*xs), color=NEWTON,
         label=fr"$\hat P(y=1\mid x)$")
x_db = -beta[0]/beta[1]
axL.axvline(x_db, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.4, label=r"boundary $\hat P=0.5$")
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$y$ / probability")
axL.set_title("Logistic fit by IRLS")
axL.legend(loc="center left", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)

axR.semilogy(range(len(hist)), np.array(hist) - min(hist) + 1e-12,
             color=NEWTON, marker="s", ms=5)
axR.set_xlabel("Newton iteration"); axR.set_ylabel("neg-log-lik $-\\ell$ above minimum")
axR.set_title("Quadratic convergence of IRLS")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"IRLS converged to beta = [{beta[0]:.3f}, {beta[1]:.3f}]  (true [0.7, 1.4])")
print(f"iterations to converge: {len(hist)-1}")
```

The Hessian here is $H=X^\top W X$ with weights $w_i=\hat p_i(1-\hat p_i)$ — *exactly* the
positive-definite "reweighted" normal-equations matrix that names IRLS. Five or six Newton
steps fit the model to machine precision, which is why every serious GLM solver is built on
this engine, not on gradient descent.

# Quasi-Newton: most of the speed, none of the Hessian {#sec-quasinewton}

Newton's bill is the Hessian. **Quasi-Newton methods** pay a fraction of it: they never form
$\nabla^2 f$, instead *building an approximation* $B_k\approx\nabla^2 f$ (or directly
$H_k\approx[\nabla^2 f]^{-1}$) from the gradients they already compute, updating it each step.
This is the most-used optimization strategy in classical (non-deep) machine learning and
scientific computing, so we develop its logic.

## The secant idea {#sec-secant}

In one dimension, you can approximate the second derivative from two *first* derivatives — a
**finite difference** of the slope:
$$
f''(x_k) \approx \frac{f'(x_k) - f'(x_{k-1})}{x_k - x_{k-1}}.
$$
This is the **secant method**: use the slope's rate of change *measured from consecutive
gradients* in place of the true second derivative. The multivariable generalization defines the
two vectors a step produces,
$$
\mathbf{s}_k = \mathbf{x}_{k+1}-\mathbf{x}_k \quad(\text{the step taken}),
\qquad
\mathbf{y}_k = \mathbf{g}_{k+1}-\mathbf{g}_k \quad(\text{how the gradient changed}),
$$
and demands that the *new* Hessian approximation reproduce this observed change — the
**secant equation**:
$$
B_{k+1}\,\mathbf{s}_k = \mathbf{y}_k.
$$ {#eq-secant}

Read it against the rearranged FTC fact $\mathbf{y}_k = \int_0^1\nabla^2 f(\cdots)\mathbf{s}_k\,dt
\approx \nabla^2 f\,\mathbf{s}_k$ from @eq-ftc-grad: the true Hessian *does* map the step
$\mathbf{s}_k$ approximately to the gradient change $\mathbf{y}_k$, so @eq-secant asks
$B_{k+1}$ to match the true Hessian's action along the one direction we just probed. One
equation cannot pin down a whole $p\times p$ matrix, so quasi-Newton methods add two further
requirements — that $B_{k+1}$ stay **symmetric**, and that it change as little as possible from
$B_k$ (a minimal update) — and the choice of "as little as possible" is what distinguishes the
methods.

## BFGS and L-BFGS {#sec-bfgs}

The **BFGS** update (Broyden–Fletcher–Goldfarb–Shanno, 1970) is the famous choice. It updates
the *inverse* Hessian approximation $H_k\approx[\nabla^2 f]^{-1}$ directly — so each step is a
cheap matrix–vector product $\mathbf{p}_k=-H_k\mathbf{g}_k$ with no linear solve at all — by the
rank-two formula
$$
H_{k+1} = \Bigl(I - \rho_k\mathbf{s}_k\mathbf{y}_k^\top\Bigr) H_k
          \Bigl(I - \rho_k\mathbf{y}_k\mathbf{s}_k^\top\Bigr) + \rho_k\mathbf{s}_k\mathbf{s}_k^\top,
\qquad \rho_k = \frac{1}{\mathbf{y}_k^\top\mathbf{s}_k}.
$$ {#eq-bfgs}

We do not re-derive the formula's coefficients from the minimal-change variational problem (it
is a constrained matrix optimization that would balloon this article past its scope); we *do*
flag exactly what it accomplishes and why it works, since that is what an interviewer and a user
need:

- It satisfies the secant equation @eq-secant by construction (multiply @eq-bfgs by
  $\mathbf{y}_k$ and the terms collapse to $\mathbf{s}_k$).
- It is a **rank-two** update — only two outer products are added — so applying it costs
  $O(p^2)$, not the $O(p^3)$ of a fresh Hessian solve.
- Crucially, if $\mathbf{y}_k^\top\mathbf{s}_k>0$ (the **curvature condition**, guaranteed by a
  line search satisfying the Wolfe conditions), the update **preserves positive-definiteness**:
  $H_{k+1}$ stays a valid, descent-producing approximation. This sidesteps the fragility of
  @sec-fragility entirely — BFGS directions are *always* descent directions.

BFGS achieves **superlinear** convergence — faster than gradient descent's linear rate, not
quite Newton's quadratic, but without ever touching a second derivative. Its cost is $O(p^2)$
per step and $O(p^2)$ memory for the dense $H_k$.

For large $p$ even $O(p^2)$ memory is too much, and **L-BFGS** ("limited-memory BFGS") is the
fix that made quasi-Newton the default for large smooth problems. Instead of storing the full
$H_k$, it stores only the last $m$ pairs $(\mathbf{s}_j,\mathbf{y}_j)$ (typically $m=5$–$20$)
and *reconstructs the action* $H_k\mathbf{g}_k$ on the fly by a clever two-loop recursion over
those pairs. Memory drops to $O(mp)$ — linear in $p$ — and each step is $O(mp)$. This is why
L-BFGS, not Newton, is what `scipy.optimize.minimize(method="L-BFGS-B")` runs by default, and
what fits a logistic regression with millions of features.

```{python}
#| label: fig-quasinewton
#| fig-cap: "Where the methods land on the speed/cost trade-off, minimizing the Rosenbrock function $f(x,y)=(1-x)^2+100(y-x^2)^2$ — the classic curved-valley benchmark, ill-conditioned and non-quadratic. The negative log of the objective (higher = more accurate) is plotted against iteration. **Newton** (red) is fastest per step but pays $O(p^3)$ and needs the Hessian. **BFGS** (green) is nearly as fast using gradients only. **Gradient descent** (grey) crawls along the curved valley floor. This is the practical ranking: Newton > quasi-Newton > first-order in *iterations*, with the cost order reversed."
def rosen(v):
    x, y = v
    return (1-x)**2 + 100*(y-x**2)**2
def rosen_g(v):
    x, y = v
    return np.array([-2*(1-x) - 400*x*(y-x**2), 200*(y-x**2)])
def rosen_H(v):
    x, y = v
    return np.array([[2 - 400*(y-3*x**2), -400*x],
                     [-400*x, 200.0]])

x0 = np.array([-1.2, 1.0])

def linesearch(v, p, g):
    t = 1.0
    while rosen(v + t*p) > rosen(v) + 1e-4*t*g@p and t > 1e-10:
        t *= 0.5
    return t

# Newton (damped + Hessian modification)
v = x0.copy(); nh = [rosen(v)]
for _ in range(25):
    g = rosen_g(v); H = rosen_H(v)
    # modify if not PD
    w = np.linalg.eigvalsh(H)
    if w.min() <= 1e-6: H = H + (1e-6 - w.min())*np.eye(2)
    p = np.linalg.solve(H, -g)
    v = v + linesearch(v, p, g)*p
    nh.append(rosen(v))

# BFGS
v = x0.copy(); B = np.eye(2); bh = [rosen(v)]; g = rosen_g(v)
for _ in range(60):
    p = -B @ g
    t = linesearch(v, p, g)
    s = t*p; v_new = v + s; g_new = rosen_g(v_new)
    yv = g_new - g
    sy = yv @ s
    if sy > 1e-10:
        rho = 1.0/sy
        I2 = np.eye(2)
        B = (I2 - rho*np.outer(s, yv)) @ B @ (I2 - rho*np.outer(yv, s)) + rho*np.outer(s, s)
    v, g = v_new, g_new
    bh.append(rosen(v))

# Gradient descent
v = x0.copy(); gh = [rosen(v)]
for _ in range(60):
    g = rosen_g(v)
    p = -g
    v = v + linesearch(v, p, g)*p
    gh.append(rosen(v))

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(range(len(nh)), -np.log10(np.maximum(nh,1e-16)), color=NEWTON, marker="s", ms=4, label="Newton")
ax.plot(range(len(bh)), -np.log10(np.maximum(bh,1e-16)), color=ACCENT, marker="o", ms=3, label="BFGS")
ax.plot(range(len(gh)), -np.log10(np.maximum(gh,1e-16)), color=GD, lw=1.5, label="gradient descent")
ax.set_xlabel("iteration"); ax.set_ylabel(r"accuracy: $-\log_{10} f$")
ax.set_title("Newton vs. BFGS vs. GD on Rosenbrock")
ax.legend(loc="lower right", framealpha=0.9)
ax.set_xlim(0, 60)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When would you use BFGS or L-BFGS instead of plain Newton, and instead of gradient
descent?"* Use a quasi-Newton method when you want most of Newton's speed but (a) the Hessian
is **expensive or unavailable** in closed form, or (b) forming/factoring it is too costly.
**BFGS** when $p$ is small enough to store a dense $p\times p$ matrix; **L-BFGS** when $p$ is
large (it stores only $O(mp)$). Use *plain Newton* when the Hessian is cheap and you want the
last drop of quadratic speed (e.g. logistic regression / GLMs, where the Hessian is the clean
$X^\top W X$). Use *gradient descent / SGD* when even $O(p^2)$ is impossible (deep nets,
$p=10^9$) or the objective is noisy/stochastic — quasi-Newton's curvature estimate is unreliable
under minibatch noise, which is why L-BFGS is rare in deep learning and SGD/Adam dominate.
:::

# Where Newton is the wrong tool {#sec-wrong-tool}

Pulling the failure threads together into a single applicability verdict, since the goal is
practitioner judgment:

- **Huge $p$ (deep learning).** $O(p^2)$ memory and $O(p^3)$ solves are impossible at
  $p=10^6$–$10^9$. Reach for SGD / Adam ([OPTM-11], [OPTM-12]); at most L-BFGS for the smaller,
  smooth, full-batch problems.
- **Stochastic / minibatch objectives.** A Hessian (or quasi-Newton curvature pair) estimated
  from a single noisy minibatch is unreliable; the second-order signal drowns in noise.
  First-order stochastic methods are the standard.
- **Non-smooth objectives.** Newton needs a Hessian; a kink ($\|\boldsymbol\beta\|_1$, a ReLU,
  a hinge loss) has none at the kink. Reach for subgradient, **proximal**, or **coordinate**
  methods ([OPTM-05], [OPTM-10], [OPTM-16]) — the lasso is solved by coordinate descent, not
  Newton.
- **Far from the optimum, badly behaved curvature.** Raw Newton can diverge; it must be
  **damped** and **modified** (@sec-fixes) to be globally safe. An undamped Newton method is a
  bug, not a method.
- **The sweet spot — where Newton wins decisively.** Smooth, $p$ small-to-medium (up to
  $\sim 10^4$), accuracy-hungry, deterministic objectives: nonlinear least squares
  (Gauss–Newton / Levenberg–Marquardt), GLM and logistic-regression MLEs (IRLS / Fisher
  scoring), and any problem where you need many correct digits in a handful of steps. Here its
  affine invariance and quadratic convergence are unmatched.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Newton's spectacular convergence is *local*.** @thm-quadratic promises quadratic convergence
only *once you are close enough* to a positive-definite minimum. Started far away, the raw
iteration can overshoot, oscillate, or diverge (@fig-divergence), and on a non-convex objective
it is *attracted to saddle points and maxima* just as readily as to minima — because all three
are roots of $\nabla f$, and Newton hunts roots of the gradient indiscriminately. Never deploy
an unguarded Newton step; the damped, Hessian-modified version (or a trust region) is the only
version that is safe in the wild.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from the descent spine — [OPTM-06 *Gradient Descent*] (the first-order step) and
[OPTM-07 *Step Size and Convergence*] (its rate, governed by the condition number $\kappa$, and
the line search we reused here for damping). This article closed the spine by adding the
**curvature**: Newton's method models the objective by a quadratic and jumps to its vertex,
buying **quadratic convergence** and **affine invariance** (no $\kappa$) at the price of forming
and factoring the Hessian.

- **Backward in the spine.** [OPTM-08 *Momentum and Accelerated Gradient Descent*] is the
  *first-order* answer to the same ill-conditioning Newton kills with curvature — momentum
  improves GD's $\kappa$-dependence without any Hessian, the cheaper middle ground.
- **Onward to scale.** [OPTM-11 *Stochastic Gradient Descent*] and [OPTM-12 *Adaptive
  Optimizers (Adam)*] are where the descent spine goes when $p$ and $n$ are too large for any
  second-order method — the regime where Newton is the *wrong* tool.
- **Constrained cousins.** The trust-region fix (@sec-fixes) is a constrained quadratic
  subproblem; the machinery is [OPTM-13 *Lagrange Multipliers*] and [OPTM-14 *KKT*].
- **What this article supplies to other tracks.** Regression [REGR-17] (logistic regression)
  and the GLM articles import the **IRLS / Fisher-scoring** engine (@sec-irls); Statistical
  Inference [INFR-B2]/[INFR-B4] import **Newton's method** for the MLE with no closed form. Both
  link here rather than re-deriving the second-order step.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The standard reference.** Nocedal & Wright, *Numerical Optimization* (2nd ed., 2006) —
  Chapters 3 (line search), 6 (quasi-Newton/BFGS), 7 (L-BFGS), and 10 (nonlinear least squares /
  Gauss–Newton). The authoritative derivations of the BFGS update and the L-BFGS two-loop
  recursion we flagged but did not expand here.
- **The convex-optimization viewpoint.** Boyd & Vandenberghe, *Convex Optimization* (2004),
  §9.5 — the cleanest treatment of Newton's method as the affine-invariant descent method, with
  the self-concordance analysis that makes the convergence *global* (not just local) for an
  important class of functions.
- **Open threads this article deferred.** *Why exactly the BFGS coefficients?* — the
  minimal-change variational derivation (Nocedal & Wright §6.1). *How does the L-BFGS two-loop
  recursion reconstruct $H_k\mathbf{g}_k$ from stored pairs?* — same source, §7.2. *Self-
  concordant functions*, where damped Newton has a fully global complexity bound independent of
  unknown constants — Boyd & Vandenberghe §9.6, the theoretical foundation of interior-point
  methods for the constrained branch ([OPTM-13]–[OPTM-16]).
